Xbox Development System Setup

Setting up your Xbox development system begins with the desktop computer that you provide. This is the computer where you will install the Xbox SDK, and it's the computer on which you will run the Microsoft® Visual C++® compiler to develop and compile Xbox executables.

If you have an earlier release of the Xbox SDK installed on your development system, updating to the latest version is the only step required for this release, so you can skip to the SDK Installation section. Otherwise, follow the steps in No Prior SDK Installed to prepare your development system for the Xbox SDK installation.

Development System Operating System Requirements

The Xbox Development Kit can be installed on Microsoft Windows 2000 or Windows XP. Be aware, however, there are known issues with using Windows 2000 on your development system. You can resolve these by installing service packs 1 through 4 for Windows 2000. You can download service packs for Windows 2000 from the Microsoft Web site at http://www.microsoft.com/windows2000/downloads/servicepacks/.

No Prior SDK Installed

  1. Your development system must be running Microsoft Windows 2000 or Windows XP, so if you have not already done so, upgrade your development system to Windows 2000 or Windows XP.
  2. If you are not doing any C++ development and do not need the code samples, you can skip the installation of Microsoft Visual Studio .NET and proceed directly to SDK Installation.
  3. Install Microsoft Visual Studio .NET Professional. For installation instructions, see the Visual Studio .NET documentation, as appropriate. This step is not necessary if a licensed copy of Visual Studio .NET is already installed on the development system. Both Visual C++ 6.0 and Visual Studio .NET may be installed on the same development system.

Note   It is possible to install the Xbox extensions for Visual Studio .NET on Visual C++ .NET Standard, but there are limitations to this configuration. Visual C++ .NET lacks the optimizing C compiler present in Visual Studio .NET and does not support attaching to a remote Xbox console (though it does support debugging on one). You may still use a development system running Visual C++ .NET for debugging and general troubleshooting, but because of the lack of an optimizing compiler, such a system is not recommended for final builds of game titles.

SDK Installation

Before you can install the SDK on Windows 2000, you must have permissions at the level of Administrator. Refer to the Windows help system for further information. Also, it is not necessary to uninstall a prior release of the Xbox SDK before installing this update. If you do not have a prior release of the Xbox SDK installed, first follow the steps in No Prior SDK Installed.

The XDK must be installed from the developer support web site (https://xds.xbox.com). Log on to the web site, download the XDK Setup executable from the XDK Release page, save it to your local hard disk, run the executable from the hard disk, and follow the instructions.

Note   When the installation program copies an updated file to the Xbox SDK directory on your hard disk, it overwrites the existing file even if you have modified that file since the last Xbox SDK installation. For example, if you made changes to the sample or tutorial code installed with a previous Xbox SDK but left those modified files in the same location and with the same filename, the update will overwrite those files with new versions and you will lose any changes you have made. Copy any modified files you wish to retain to a new location before installing the Xbox SDK.

Establishing a Network Connection Between the Xbox Console and the Development System

If you are installing the Xbox SDK for the first time on this development system, or have changed the machine name of the Xbox console, you must set up the network connection between the console and the development system. After completing all of the steps above and all of the steps in Xbox Console Setup, and with both systems running, follow these steps:

  1. From the development system, click the Start button, click Run, and type "cmd" in the Open: window that appears.

  2. From the C:\ prompt in the cmd.exe window, type "xbdir -x name xe:\", where name is the name of your Xbox console entered during the Installing the Xbox System Software on the Xbox Console step. In place of a name that you assign, you may enter the IP address of the Xbox console (for example, 123.45.67.89). The IP address is displayed at the bottom of the XDK Launcher.

    Notes

    • If the Xbox console and the development system are not on the same subnet, you must enter the IP address instead of the Xbox console name.
    • If you have not successfully completed steps 1 and 2 and attempt to build an Xbox executable, a No address for Xbox has been set compiler error message will result.
    • You can use the "xbdir xe:\" command at any time to verify that the two systems are communicating.

To Uninstall the Xbox SDK

You do not need to follow these steps unless you want to uninstall the Xbox SDK.

  1. Launch the Add/Remove Programs dialog box from the Windows Control Panel, select Microsoft Xbox SDK, and then click Change/Remove.
  2. Setup will detect the previous installation of the Xbox SDK. Follow the instructions on the screen.

Note   Uninstalling the Xbox SDK may not delete all files installed with the Xbox SDK. To complete uninstallation in such a case, manually delete the Xbox SDK root folder (located at C:\Program Files\Microsoft Xbox SDK, by default).

To uninstall Visual C++ or Visual Studio .NET, see the Visual C++ or Visual Studio .NET documentation.
